title: Solar cooking with a parabolic reflector
subtitle: How parabolic solar cookers work, UK solar resource, and building or buying a basic unit
excerpt: A parabolic solar cooker concentrates sunlight onto a cooking vessel, reaching 150-200°C in clear sunshine. In the UK, reliable solar cooking is possible from April to September in most of England and Wales, with 4-6 usable solar hours on a clear summer day. A basic commercial cooker costs a modest cost; a DIY version from cardboard and kitchen foil costs around a modest cost.
sourceNotes: Solar Energy International parabolic solar cooker design guides. Aprovecho Research Center (USA) solar cooker performance testing. UK Met Office solar radiation data by region. Solar Cookers International wiki for DIY designs. Gov.uk PVGIS solar irradiance tool for UK location-specific data.

content.text: A parabolic solar cooker is a concave mirror shaped to focus sunlight onto a single point, where a dark-coloured cooking pot sits. In full sunshine, the focal point reaches 150-300°C depending on the reflector size. A 1.2 m diameter dish generates roughly 200-400 W of thermal power at the pot, equivalent to a small electric hob ring. In UK conditions, this cooks a pot of rice in 15-25 minutes, boils 1 litre of water in 10-15 minutes, and slow-cooks beans or stew over 2-3 hours on a clear summer day.

Solar cooking requires direct sunshine (not diffuse overcast light). The UK has 1,400 to 1,800 sunshine hours each year in England and Wales, falling to 1,100 to 1,300 each year in Scotland. Useful direct-beam solar cooking hours concentrate in April to September, typically 4-6 hours per clear day from 10 am to 4 pm (BST). Cloud cover makes UK solar cooking unreliable as a daily cooking method but viable as a fuel-free supplement on clear summer days. The PVGIS online tool (European Commission) gives monthly direct normal irradiance data for any UK postcode.

The focal point of a parabolic cooker is intense enough to cause immediate skin burns and eye damage. Never look directly at the reflector surface when it is aimed at the sun. Wear dark non-reflective sunglasses when adjusting the cooker. Keep children at least 2 m away from the focal zone during cooking.
Parabolic: highest temperature, requires tracking (re-aiming at the sun every 20-30 minutes). Best for boiling and frying. Commercial designs from a modest cost (SunFocus, SunCook).

Box cooker: an insulated box with a reflective lid that focuses diffuse light in. Lower temperature (80-120°C) but works in partly cloudy conditions. Best for slow cooking, baking, and pasteurising water. More practical for UK weather.

Panel cooker (e.g., CooKit design): simple flat reflective panels arranged around a pot in a black bag or glass bowl. Low cost, medium temperature (80-150°C), portable. Can be made from cardboard and kitchen foil for around a modest cost.

Use a dark (matte black or dark-coloured enamel) pot with a lid. Shiny metal pots reflect the focused sunlight away from the pot surface and absorb heat poorly. A dark pot in the focal zone of a 1.2 m parabolic cooker absorbs 70-80% of the incoming solar radiation. Avoid lifting the lid during cooking: steam retention is important at the lower temperatures typical of solar cooking.
